title: Apply: The Compromises at the Convention
description: Apply your knowledge about the compromises the delegates made during the Constitutional Convention.
topic_path: The Road to Reform, the Constitutional Convention, and the Battle for Ratification
A Compromise with Evil: Slavery at the Constitutional Convention
Apply: The Compromises at the Convention

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Madison’s Virginia Plan, which he presented at the Constitutional Convention.**

1. Resolved that the Articles of Confederation ought to be so corrected & enlarged as to accomplish the [[☃ definition 1]] proposed by their [[☃ definition 2]]; namely, "common defense, security of liberty and general welfare."

2. Resolved therefore that the rights of suffrage in the National Legislature ought to be proportioned to the [[☃ definition 3]], or to the number of free inhabitants, as the one or the other rule may seem best in different cases.

3. Resolved that the National Legislature ought to consist of two branches.

**Based on the excerpt, what was one goal of the Virginia Plan?**
[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: amounts of money that each state paid to cover the expenses of the national government

choices.content: To give states the power to veto national laws that violated state laws and constitutions
To give the national government the power to address problems that affected the nation
To give the right to vote to people who hadn’t previously been considered citizens
To give the judicial and executive branches the power to check the legislative branch

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Madison’s Virginia Plan, which he presented at the Constitutional Convention.**

1. Resolved that the Articles of Confederation ought to be so corrected & enlarged as to accomplish the [[☃ definition 1]] proposed by their [[☃ definition 2]]; namely, "common defense, security of liberty and general welfare."

2. Resolved therefore that the rights of suffrage in the National Legislature ought to be proportioned to the [[☃ definition 3]], or to the number of free inhabitants, as the one or the other rule may seem best in different cases.

3. Resolved that the National Legislature ought to consist of two branches.

**Based on the excerpt, what change did Madison’s Virginia Plan propose for the legislative branch?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: amounts of money that each state paid to cover the expenses of the national government

choices.content: Congress would be divided into two houses.
Congress would include one representative for each state.
Congress would only be able to legislate when the states could not agree.
Congress would no longer have any power to help defend the nation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Alexander Hamilton’s speech at the Constitutional Convention, as described in Robert Yates’s notes. Hamilton is discussing the New Jersey Plan**

Examine the present confederation, and it is evident they can raise no troops nor equip vessels before war is actually declared. They cannot therefore take any preparatory measure before an enemy is at your door. How unwise and inadequate their powers! and this must ever be the case when you attempt to define powers. Something will always be [[☃ definition 1]]. 

Congress, by being annually elected, and subject to recall, will ever come with the prejudices of their states rather than the good of the union. Add therefore additional powers to a body thus organized, and you establish a sovereignty of the worst kind, consisting of a single body. Where are the checks? None. They must either [[☃ definition 4]] over the state governments, or the [[☃ definition 3]] of the state governments must end in their [[☃ definition 2]]. This is a conclusive objection to the [New] Jersey plan.

**Which statement best summarizes Hamilton’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The New Jersey Plan will work because representatives who are elected annually will be responsive to the concerns of the states.
The New Jersey Plan will work because it gives Congress all the powers it does not currently have under the Articles of Confederation.
The New Jersey Plan will not work because it does not include the necessary checks and balances.
The New Jersey Plan will not work because small states will have no say in the laws Congress passes under proportional representation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Alexander Hamilton’s speech at the Constitutional Convention, as described in Robert Yates’s notes. Hamilton is discussing the New Jersey Plan**

Examine the present confederation, and it is evident they can raise no troops nor equip vessels before war is actually declared. They cannot therefore take any preparatory measure before an enemy is at your door. How unwise and inadequate their powers! and this must ever be the case when you attempt to define powers. Something will always be [[☃ definition 1]]. 

Congress, by being annually elected, and subject to recall, will ever come with the prejudices of their states rather than the good of the union. Add therefore additional powers to a body thus organized, and you establish a sovereignty of the worst kind, consisting of a single body. Where are the checks? None. They must either [[☃ definition 2]] over the state governments, or the [[☃ definition 3]] of the state governments must end in their [[☃ definition 4]]. This is a conclusive objection to the [New] Jersey plan.

**According to Hamilton, what was one problem with the way the New Jersey Plan structured Congress?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It gave the larger states more representation in Congress than the smaller states.
It called for representatives to be elected infrequently, which would allow them to ignore their state’s concerns.
It dissolved state governments, as Congress gained too much power.
It made Congress too accountable to the states, which was not in the best interest of the nation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Gouverneur Morris shared the following remarks at the Constitutional Convention. This is an excerpt from James Madison’s notes.**

Upon what principle is it that the slaves shall be computed in the representation? Are they men? Then make them citizens, and let them vote. Are they property? Why, then, is no other property included? The houses in [[☃ definition 1]] are worth more than all the wretched slaves who cover the rice swamps of South Carolina.

**Based on the passage, what was Morris objecting to in his remarks?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Including a provision that would end the international slave trade in 1808
Prohibiting the extension of slavery into new territories under the Northwest Ordinance
Granting formerly enslaved people citizenship based on their contributions to the economy
Counting enslaved people as part of a state’s population for representation in Congress

assessment_items.question_content:
**Gouverneur Morris shared the following remarks at the Constitutional Convention. This is an excerpt from James Madison’s notes.**

Upon what principle is it that the slaves shall be computed in the representation? Are they men? Then make them citizens, and let them vote. Are they property? Why, then, is no other property included? The houses in [[☃ definition 1]] are worth more than all the wretched slaves who cover the rice swamps of South Carolina.

**Which compromise at the Constitutional Convention was meant to address the kinds of concerns that Morris raises in his remarks?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: For every ten enslaved persons, five would be counted for the purposes of representation.
For every five enslaved persons, three would be counted for the purposes of representation.
Every enslaved person would be counted toward a state’s population.
No enslaved person would be counted as part of a state’s population.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Edmund Randolph shared the following remarks at the Constitutional Convention about the Article I, Section 9— the Slave Trade Clause. This is an excerpt from James Madison’s notes.**

Mr. Randolph was for [[☃ definition 1]] in order that some middle ground might, if possible, be found. He could never agree to the clause as it stands. He [would] sooner risk the constitution. He dwelt on the dilemma to which the Convention was exposed. By agreeing to the clause, it would revolt the [[☃ definition 2]], the Methodists, and many others in the States having no slaves. On the other hand, two States might be lost to the Union. Let us then, he said, try the chance of a commitment.

**Which statement best summarizes Randolph’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: sending a proposal to a committee for consideration
a religious group opposed to slavery

choices.content: The convention needs to find a compromise between the pro-slavery delegates and the anti-slavery delegates.
The convention needs to require each state to allow slavery;  otherwise, southern states will not agree to the new constitution.
The convention needs to include a provision that eventually ends slavery; otherwise, the anti-slavery states will not agree to the new constitution.
The convention should not include any provisions about slavery because the practice will eventually die out on its own.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Edmund Randolph shared the following remarks at the Constitutional Convention about the Article I, Section 9— the Slave Trade Clause. This is an excerpt from James Madison’s notes.**

Mr. Randolph was for [[☃ definition 1]] in order that some middle ground might, if possible, be found. He could never agree to the clause as it stands. He [would] sooner risk the constitution. He dwelt on the dilemma to which the Convention was exposed. By agreeing to the clause, it would revolt the [[☃ definition 2]], the Methodists, and many others in the States having no slaves. On the other hand, two States might be lost to the Union. Let us then, he said, try the chance of a commitment.

**In the passage, Randolph calls for finding a “middle ground.” Which statement best describes how the delegates found this middle ground?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: sending a proposal to a committee for consideration
a religious group opposed to slavery

choices.content: They decided that enslaved people would count for representation, but the practice of slavery would end in 1865.
They decided that enslaved people would not count for representation, but enslaved people who escaped would be returned to their enslavers.
They decided enslaved people would count for representation, but Congress would have the power to end the international slave trade in 1808.
They decided enslaved people would not count for representation, but slavery would not be outlawed in the Constitution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1787 letter to the head of Congress from George Washington, who served as the president of the Constitutional Convention.**

It is obviously [[☃ definition 1]] in the federal government of these States to secure all rights of independent sovereignty to each, and yet provide for the interest and safety of all—Individuals entering into society, must give up a share of liberty to preserve the rest. The [[☃ definition 2]] of the sacrifice must depend as well on situation and circumstance, as on the object to be obtained. It is at all times difficult to draw with precision the line between those rights which must be surrendered, and those which may be reserved; and on the present occasion this difficulty was increased by a difference among the [[☃ definition 3]] States as to their situation, extent, habits, and particular interests.

**This excerpt can be used as evidence of which goal of the Constitutional Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To maintain a confederal form of government where the states had much more power than the federal government
To empower the federal government to achieve common purposes of the nation, like national security
To link the thirteen colonies in an alliance to prevent foreign powers from taking control over individual colonies
To list the individual rights and liberties of citizens that the federal government cannot infringe upon

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a set of notes on Charles Cotesworth Pinckney’s speech at the South Carolina Ratification Convention in 1788.**

. . . With regard to the objection that had been made to the [[☃ definition 1]] of electing the president of the United States, Gen. Pinckney asked what other mode would have been so proper? If he was to be elected by the house of representatives and the senate, as one of them have the power of impeaching and the other of trying him, he would be altogether their [[☃ definition 2]], and would not have independence enough to exercise with firmness, the revisionary power and other authorities with which he is invested by the constitution? This [[☃ definition 3]] of independence might influence his conduct in some degree if he was to be elected by one branch of the legislature alone; but as he is to be elected by the people, through the medium of electors chosen particularly for that purpose, and he is in some measure to be a check on the senate and house of representatives, the election, in my opinion, could not have been placed so well if it had been made in any other mode.

**Which statement best summarizes Pinckney’s argument in the speech?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: someone who is dependent on someone else

choices.content: The Electoral College is the best way to elect a president, as it leads to the election of a president who isn’t obligated to the legislature.
The use of the Electoral College to choose the winner of the presidency will weaken the people’s authority to decide who should become president.
The Electoral College is the most effective method of electing the president, as electors are chosen by representatives in the legislative branch.
The use of the Electoral College to determine the president will allow larger states to have more influence than smaller states over the presidency.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “Reason for Dissent,” written by Malachi Maynard and Samuel Field under the [[☃ definition 2]] “Consider Arms.”**

In the first place—as direct taxes are to be [[☃ definition 1]] according to the numbers in each state, and as Massachusetts has none in it but [those who] are declared freemen, so the whole, blacks as well as whites, must be numbered; this must therefore operate against us, as two fifths of the slaves in the southern states are to be left out of the [[☃ definition 3]]; consequently, three Massachusetts infants will increase the tax equal to five sturdy, full grown negroes of theirs, who work every day in the week for their masters, [[☃ definition 4]] the Sabbath, upon which they are allowed to get something for their own support. We can see no justice in this way of apportioning taxes; neither can we see any good reason why this was consented to on the part of our delegates.

**Which part of the Constitution are the authors critiquing in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Third Amendment
The Fifth Amendment
The Fugitive Slave Clause
The Three-Fifths Clause

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the sixth article in the Northwest Ordinance of 1787, which was published before the ratification of the U.S. Constitution.**

There shall be neither slavery nor involuntary servitude in the said territory otherwise than in punishment of crimes whereof the party shall have been duly convicted: provided always that any person escaping into the same from whom labor or service is lawfully claimed in any one of the original states, such fugitive may be lawfully reclaimed and [[☃ definition 1]] to the person claiming his, or her labor, or service as aforesaid.

**The language in the excerpt most directly reflects the language of which part of the U.S. Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Three-Fifths Clause
The Supremacy Clause
The Fugitive Slave Clause
The Slave Trade Clause
